questions.question: Explain the difference between a Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) and a CUDA core. How do they relate to GPU parallelism?
keyPoints: SM is the fundamental processing unit containing multiple CUDA cores
CUDA cores are individual arithmetic logic units (ALUs) that execute instructions
Multiple threads run concurrently on an SM through warp scheduling
An SM can execute multiple warps simultaneously through time-slicing
GPU parallelism comes from having many SMs, each running many threads

questions.question: Describe the memory hierarchy in a CUDA GPU, listing at least three different memory types and their characteristics.
keyPoints: Global memory: largest capacity, highest latency, accessible by all threads
Shared memory: small capacity, low latency, shared within a block
Registers: fastest, per-thread storage, limited availability
Local memory: off-chip, used when registers overflow
Constant memory: read-only, cached for broadcast access

questions.question: What is memory coalescing? Why is it important for GPU performance? Provide an example of coalesced vs non-coalesced access pattern.
keyPoints: Coalesced access: consecutive threads access consecutive memory addresses
Enables single memory transaction for multiple requests
Improves bandwidth utilization significantly
Example coalesced: C[i] = A[i] + B[i] for all i
Example non-coalsced: C[i] = A[i*stride] where stride ≠ block size

questions.question: Explain the thread-block-bgrid model in CUDA. How do you determine the appropriate block size and grid size for a kernel?
keyPoints: Threads are grouped into blocks, blocks form a grid
Block size chosen based on: resources per block, parallelism per block
Grid size = ceil(N/blockSize) for N elements
Consider SM capacity: registers, shared memory per block
Trade-off between occupancy and resource utilization

questions.question: What is warp divergence and how does it affect GPU performance? Suggest one method to minimize warp divergence.
keyPoints: Warp divergence: threads in same warp take different paths due to conditionals
Results in serialized execution of different code paths
Reduces effective parallelism within the warp
Minimization: restructure code to avoid branching within warps
Use predicated execution or loop restructuring to reduce divergence

questions.question: Write a CUDA kernel for matrix multiplication (C = A × B) where A and B are N×N matrices. Assume matrices are stored in row-major order. Discuss the optimization strategies you would apply to improve performance.
keyPoints: Basic kernel: each thread computes one element C[i][j]
Load appropriate rows from A and columns from B
Optimizations: use shared memory for tile-based multiplication
Coalesced global memory access for rows of A and columns of B
Register usage, warp alignment, occupancy considerations

questions.question: Compare and contrast CPU and GPU architectures for parallel computing. Under what circumstances would you choose GPU computing over CPU computing? Provide examples of applications suitable for each.
keyPoints: CPU: fewer cores, complex control logic, high single-thread performance
GPU: thousands of simple cores, optimized for throughput
GPU suitable for: data-parallel, throughput-oriented workloads
Examples GPU: deep learning, image processing, scientific simulations
Examples CPU: task-parallel, complex branching, low-latency requirements

questions.question: Design a CUDA program to perform parallel reduction (sum of all elements in an array). Explain the algorithm, the kernel implementation, and analyze its time complexity. How would you optimize this for modern GPUs?
keyPoints: Reduction: binary tree pattern combining adjacent elements
Parallel reduction: each thread pairs and adds elements
Multiple phases reducing array size by half each iteration
Time complexity: O(log n) with n processors
Optimizations: bank conflict avoidance, warp-level primitives
Shared memory usage, loop unrolling for last warp
